title: Why 3x3 Filters Became a Standard
concept: Stacked 3x3 convolutions became a common CNN pattern because they expand receptive field gradually, insert extra nonlinear layers, and keep architecture blocks modular and reusable.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which filter size is the signature building block in VGG?
exercises.explain: Yes — VGG is famous for leaning hard on repeated 3x3 convolutions.

cards.title: One brick size is easy to reuse
cards.body: If every stage uses the same kind of small brick, you can build, test, and extend the network without constantly changing the recipe. That is part of why 3x3 became so popular.

cards.title: Why 3x3 works so well
cards.body: A 3x3 convolution is large enough to capture local patterns like corners or short edges, but small enough to stack cleanly. When you repeat it, the receptive field grows layer by layer, and each extra layer can add another ReLU or normalization step.

cards.title: LEGO bricks vs. giant carved blocks
cards.body: A giant carved block gives one big piece right away, but many standard LEGO bricks are easier to combine in different ways. Stacked 3x3 filters give CNN designers that same modular freedom.

cards.title: Growing context without one huge kernel
cards.body: Three 3x3 layers can cover a roughly 7x7 view. If the input and output channel counts stay similar, that stack can also use fewer learned weights than one 7x7 convolution — while adding more nonlinear processing steps.

cards.title: 3x3 is common, not magic
cards.body: Do not turn this into a superstition. CNNs still use 1x1, 5x5, dilated, grouped, or depthwise filters when the job calls for them. 3x3 became a standard because it is a strong default, not because every other filter is wrong.

cards.title: Same wider goal, different path
cards.body: Both designs can reach a bigger view, but the stacked route stays modular.

left.text:
Wide context in one layer
One big kernel choice
Fewer stages to mix features

right.text:
Roughly 7x7 context overall
Extra nonlinear steps
Reusable block pattern

exercises.prompt: Why did stacked 3x3 filters become such a common CNN design choice?

options.text: They grow receptive field gradually while keeping the architecture modular
They remove the need for activations like ReLU
They guarantee the network will be tiny
They only work on black-and-white images

exercises.explain: Exactly — stacked 3x3 layers balance local detail, wider context, and repeatable design.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Several 3x3 layers can create a larger effective view while also inserting extra nonlinear steps between them.
exercises.explain: True — that mix of wider context and extra processing depth is a big reason the pattern lasted.

exercises.sentence: A big reason 3x3 filters spread is that they make CNN blocks more ___.
exercises.explain: Modular is right — designers can repeat the same block shape across many stages.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best explains why many CNNs copied the 3x3 pattern after VGG?

options.text: It gives a clean default block that can widen context through depth instead of one oversized filter
It proves larger kernels can never work in vision models
It turns every CNN into a transformer automatically

exercises.explain: Right — the win is not a magic number, but a practical, repeatable design pattern.

exercises.prompt: You can either use one 7x7 convolution or stack three 3x3 convolutions. Why might a CNN designer prefer the stacked option?

options.text: Because the stacked 3x3 path can reach a similar wider view while staying modular and adding extra nonlinear stages
Because 7x7 filters are illegal in CNNs
Because stacked 3x3 layers never use any computation
Because only single-layer networks can use big kernels

exercises.explain: Exactly — the stacked small-filter route is popular because it is expressive, modular, and easy to repeat across a network.
